answer: The Nile is generally considered the longest river at 6,650 km, but some sources list the Amazon at 6,992 km, making it longer by 342 km. The Amazon's volume is significantly greater, carrying four times more water than the Nile.

results.title: Top 10 longest rivers in the world 2023 | BBC Science Focus
results.snippet: # Top 10 longest rivers in the world 2024. The tenth-longest river in the world is the Amur River, which extends 4,444km (2,761 miles). The Congo River is the ninth-longest in the world and second-longest in Africa, extending a total distance of 4,700km (2,922 miles) from its farthest source to its mouth. Río de la Plata is the eighth-longest river in the world and it measures a grand 4,880km (3,032 miles) in length. The seventh-longest river in the world is the Ob, which extends a total of 5,410km (3,362 miles). The Yellow River is the sixth-longest river in the world, stretching 5,464km (3,395 miles) across China. The fifth-longest river in the world is the Yenisey, which extends 5,539km (3,445 miles) north through Mongolia and Russia. The second-longest river in the world is the Amazon, which stretches at least 6,400km (3,977 miles) from source to mouth. The Nile is the longest river in the world and measures 6,695km (4,160 miles) in length from its source to its mouth.

results.title: What’s the world’s longest river? A new Amazon expedition aims to answer that question once and for all | CNN
results.snippet: “The Nile is like a worm and the Amazon is an anaconda,” 55-year-old Brazilian expedition leader Yuri Sanada, a seasoned explorer and film producer, recounts of his colleague’s metaphorical take on the Amazon’s volume, which carries four times more water than any other river. Sanada says Contos’s findings offer the expedition team an “excuse to be there,” explaining that while mapping the river is its ostensible goal, the expedition has greater aims of more fully documenting and shining a global spotlight on the Amazon rainforest region’s rich biodiversity – and the need for the worldwide community to work together to help conserve it. Sanada says that in early 2025 there will be a secondary expedition starting at the Apurimac River in Peru, the traditionally accepted source of the Amazon, which will allow for a second set of measurements.
